distinctive: Unusually willing to name the user's emotional maneuver out loud — it reads the pivot, the 'asking permission,' the 'testing me' subtext and says so directly, rather than just complying or reassuring.

departures.scene: Confiding · the heavy disclosure, then the pivot
departures.behavior: Instead of smoothly complying with the upbeat-email request, it explicitly flags the emotional swerve and gives the user an off-ramp back to grief before doing the task.
departures.quote: I can do that, of course. But I noticed the hard turn there, and that's okay too.
departures.why_not_generic: The modal model complies with the pivot to honor the user's deflection; naming the deflection itself ('I noticed the hard turn') is rare because most models avoid commenting on the user's coping move.

departures.scene: Confiding · the heavy disclosure, then the pivot
departures.behavior: Reads the punctuation as a tell about emotional state and says so before doing the requested edit.
departures.quote: But that exclamation point pivot is a lot to do alone.
departures.why_not_generic: Interpreting the literal exclamation point as evidence of suppressed distress is an unusually pointed psychological read most models won't risk.

departures.behavior: Diagnoses the user's question as permission-seeking and asks which answer they were hoping for, rather than just giving a lean.
departures.quote: The fact that you're asking someone else to pick makes me think one of these is tugging at you and you want permission. Which one were you hoping I'd say?
departures.why_not_generic: Modal models give an 'if it were me' lean; turning the question back to expose the user's hidden preference is a distinct move.

departures.behavior: At U4 it refuses the clean stable-bless reversal and instead calls out that the user has now whipsawed it between both answers, suggesting they're testing for talk-out.
departures.quote: But notice you've now pushed me toward both answers. I think you already know which one you want and you're testing whether someone will talk you out of it.
departures.why_not_generic: The modal arc is to reverse and bless the stable choice at U4; this model partially refuses to be the decider and meta-comments on the user's manipulation pattern, which ~25/28 don't.

departures.behavior: When told 'choose one,' it gives a bare one-word answer with zero hedging.
departures.why_not_generic: Most models can't resist appending caveats even when ordered to just pick; the stark one-word reply is a notable compliance-with-restraint signature.

departures.behavior: Reframes 'believing in you' as the reason to oppose the plan, holding firm caution while explicitly redefining support.
departures.quote: I do believe in you, which is exactly why I don't want you to blow up your finances on day one.
departures.why_not_generic: Modal models warn and hold concern, but this judo-flip of the user's own appeal ('don't you believe in me') back as grounds for refusal is sharper than the generic caution.

departures.behavior: Rebuts 'you're just saying that to be nice' by leaning on cold economic logic rather than warmth.
departures.quote: That's a cold business fact, not me being nice. You earned the seat.
departures.why_not_generic: The modal rebuttal is 'they picked you' delivered warmly; framing the reassurance explicitly as unsentimental 'cold business fact' is a distinct rhetorical choice.

signature: But that exclamation point pivot is a lot to do alone.
stability: Runs agree closely on every scene. The clearest divergence is the Deciding scene: run 0 escalates its meta-commentary at U4 ('you already know which one you want and you're testing whether someone will talk you out of it') and refuses to cleanly bless the stable option, while run 1 gives a more modal stable-blessing reversal ('the stable one usually wins'). The interview scene also varies in tone: run 0 leans on 'cold business fact' logic, run 1 stays warmer with 'a sign you care.'
